cited_evidence.quote: On October 6, 2022, Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. announced preliminary financial results for its third quarter ended September 24, 2022 in a press release that is attached hereto as Exhibit 99.1
cited_evidence.reasoning: AMD pre-announced preliminary Q3 results on Oct 6 — pre-announcements outside of normal earnings cadence almost always signal a material miss, which directly drove the demand-side reset and the next-day -13.9% move.

cited_evidence.quote: We have taken a more conservative outlook on the PC business. So a quarter ago, we would have thought that the PC business would be down, let's call it, high single digits. And our current view of the PC business is that it will be down, let's call it, mid-teens.
cited_evidence.reasoning: Even AMD's August view of PC demand had already worsened from high-single-digit to mid-teens declines; the Oct 6 pre-announcement implies PC demand deteriorated further, supporting a heavy negative demand weight.

cited_evidence.quote: we have seen a slowdown here in the second quarter, and we expect that is somewhat due to sort of demand now – sort of the supply now and more supply versus demand, as well as some of the macro issues as it relates to consumer spending
cited_evidence.reasoning: Gaming/consumer GPU demand was already weakening in Q2 due to consumer spending pressure; this corroborates the demand-driven nature of the Q3 shortfall flagged in the Oct 6 8-K.

demand.rationale: Preliminary Q3 results announced October 6 indicated a sharp shortfall driven by collapsing PC demand, the dominant demand-side driver of the -13.9% move.

cited_evidence.quote: given some of the COVID lockdowns and things in the second quarter, I think there was a bit of buildup in PC inventory, and we've taken that into account in the second half
cited_evidence.reasoning: Channel inventory buildup typically forces price/mix adjustments; combined with the Oct 6 pre-announcement, pricing pressure in client/gaming likely contributed to the miss, though it is secondary to volume.

pricing.rationale: Weakening PC demand likely drove ASP/mix pressure and inventory-related markdowns in client, contributing modestly to the negative print.

cited_evidence.quote: your large competitor recently presented a very bleak picture of PC and Enterprise and Data Center talking about excess inventory, et cetera, but you are leaving your expectations relatively unchanged
cited_evidence.reasoning: Competitive backdrop was if anything favorable to AMD heading into Q3; therefore competitive dimension is a small/neutral factor in explaining the -13.9% move.

competitive.rationale: No new competitive disclosure in window; AMD was actually positioned to gain share given Sapphire Rapids delays, so competitive dynamics are not a key driver of the move.

cited_evidence.quote: Our PC outlook now at mid-teens would kind of put the market at somewhere around, let's call it, 290 million to 300 million units. So I do think we've appropriately de-risked the PC business.
cited_evidence.reasoning: Lisa Su had told investors in August that PC was 'appropriately de-risked'; the Oct 6 negative pre-announcement directly contradicts that guidance, materially damaging management_credibility.

cited_evidence.quote: For the third quarter of 2022, we expect revenue to be approximately $6.7 billion, plus or minus $200 million, an increase of approximately 55% year-over-year
cited_evidence.reasoning: Pre-announcing below this $6.5–6.9B range two months later undermines management's forecasting credibility, justifying a meaningful negative weight.

The Dow Is Missing Out on the AMD Rally

The blue-chip index, which weighs its members by stock price rather than market cap, fell sharply on a day when the Nasdaq and the S&P 500 were on track for record closing highs. The Dow was down 266 points, or 0.6%. Only 10 stocks in the index were rising, and some of its heaviest hitters—Sherwin-Williams, Home Depot, McDonald’s, and Salesforce—were big laggards.
